metadata.title: Five Whys: Find Your Real Reason for Being in College

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Shalom, Liam — in for Bear. Build it with a CLI, then take it apart? Can you explain it, Bear?

props.command: Build it with a CLI, then take it apart?
props.segment: Five Whys: Find Your Real Reason

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. Build it with a CLI, then take it apart.

beats.role_note: the problem, the context, the stakes — BEFORE the CLI
beats.narration_text: Most students who drop out in the first year do not lack ability. They lack a reason that holds under pressure. When the semester gets hard — and it will get hard — the students who stay are the ones who know why they are there at a level deeper than 'get a degree' or 'my parents said so.' The Five Whys is a root-cause technique borrowed from manufacturing that finds that deeper level in five questions.
beats.visual_intent: title card: 'Why do you stay when it gets hard?' — question on screen before CLI opens; no terminal yet

beats.narration_text: One command. Ask Claude to run the Five Whys as an interactive dialogue — it asks why you're in college, then follows your answer with four more questions, each drilling deeper. After five rounds, it summarizes what each round revealed.

props.command: claude "I want to do the Five Whys exercise to understand my reason for being in college. Start by asking me 'Why are you in college?' then ask a follow-up question based on my answer for each of 5 rounds. After 5 rounds, summarize what each round revealed — what the fifth answer implies about my underlying motivation."

beats.narration_text: The output is a transcript — five underscore whys underscore transcript dot md. Claude starts with the question, records the answer, and each round the question narrows. By round five the surface answer — 'get a job' — has been replaced by something much more specific and much more durable.

props.code:
# five_whys_transcript.md — round-by-round dialogue output

## Round 1
Q: Why are you in college?
A: To get a good job.

## Round 2
Q: Why do you want that specific kind of job?
A: I want financial stability for my family.

## Round 3
Q: Why is financial stability for your family the goal?
A: My parents sacrificed a lot — I want to close that gap.

## Round 4
Q: Why does closing that gap matter to you right now?
A: Because they're not getting younger and I feel the window.

## Round 5
Q: Why does the window closing create urgency for you?
A: Because I want them to see it happen, not just hear about it.

## Summary
Round 1 named an outcome. Round 5 named a person and a deadline.
Underlying motivation: time-bound obligation to specific people.

beats.narration_text: The transcript lands. Five rounds, each one a layer down. The first answer was generic enough to belong to anyone. The fifth answer belongs to exactly one person. That is the answer that holds under a hard semester.
beats.visual_intent: screen recording of the five-round transcript in terminal — round 5 highlighted, summary visible at bottom

beats.narration_text: Now make it actionable. Ask Claude to take the underlying motivation from round five and write a one-sentence personal mission statement plus three concrete behaviors that would express that motivation in a typical college week.

props.command: claude "Take the underlying motivation from round 5 of the Five Whys — time-bound obligation to specific people. Write: (1) a one-sentence personal mission statement, (2) three concrete weekly behaviors that express this motivation in a college setting."

beats.narration_text: The revised output converts the root motivation into a statement you can read at the start of every week, and three behaviors you can actually check: study session logged, one faculty contact made, weekly progress note sent.
beats.visual_intent: screen recording of mission statement and three behavioral commitments — specific, checkable, tied to the root motivation from round 5

beats.narration_text: The Five Whys is a root-cause tool. In manufacturing it finds why a machine failed. In college it finds why you showed up. The fifth answer is always more specific and more durable than the first — and it is the one that actually keeps you in the building when the semester turns.
beats.visual_intent: recap card: 'Round 1 = outcome. Round 5 = person + deadline.' The five rounds as a ladder graphic

beats.narration_text: Your move: run this exercise yourself — not as a thought experiment, as a typed conversation. Save the transcript. Read round five before your next hard week. And if the answer changes over the semester, run it again — a motivation that evolves is still a motivation.
beats.visual_intent: next-steps card: 1) run the Five Whys with Claude, 2) save round 5 answer, 3) re-run each semester

The fifth answer is always more specific and more durable than the first — and it is the one that actually keeps you in the building when the semester turns. Your move: run this exercise yourself — not as a thought experiment, as a typed conversation. Save the transcript. Read round five before your next hard week. And if the answer changes over the semester, run it again — a motivation that evolves is still a motivation.

beats.title: Your turn handoff — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.

props.command: Explain how [Five Whys: Find Your Real Reason for Being in College] applies to a specific cancer type or clinical case you're studying. What proteins are involved, what goes wrong at the molecular level, and what is the therapeutic or diagnostic significance?
